title: Add voice call support to the existing Plivo notification provider
author: sarveshpatil-plivo
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

This builds directly on the already-merged Plivo SMS provider #7584. It is additive: same provider, adds an optional Voice Call mode, and SMS behavior is unchanged so existing configurations keep working. I did not find any other open issue or pull request proposing voice-call support for the Plivo provider.

### 🔖 Feature description

The Plivo notification provider (added in #7584) currently sends SMS. This proposes adding an optional Voice Call mode, so a monitor alert can also place a phone call via Plivo's Voice API. It is useful for higher-severity/on-call escalation where an SMS may be missed.

### ✔️ Solution

A "Message Type" toggle on the existing Plivo provider: SMS (current behavior, unchanged and still the default) or Voice Call.
In Voice Call mode the provider places a call via the Plivo Call API using a user-supplied Answer URL (a Plivo Application that returns Plivo XML). The alert text is passed as a ?message= query parameter so the answer XML can speak it.

### ❓ Alternatives

_No response_

### 📝 Additional Context

Opening this for discussion first (per the contribution guide) before raising the PR. The implementation is ready; it extends server/notification-providers/plivo.js, the Plivo.vue form, and src/lang/en.json. Happy to adjust the approach.

title: Add voice call support to the Plivo notification provider
author: sarveshpatil-plivo
body:
**Feature description**
The Plivo notification provider (added in #7584) currently sends SMS. This proposes adding an optional Voice Call mode, so a monitor alert can also place a phone call via Plivo's Voice API. It is useful for higher-severity/on-call escalation where an SMS may be missed.

**How it would work**
A "Message Type" toggle on the existing Plivo provider: SMS (current behavior, unchanged and still the default) or Voice Call.
In Voice Call mode the provider places a call via the Plivo Call API using a user-supplied Answer URL (a Plivo Application that returns Plivo XML). The alert text is passed as a `?message=` query parameter so the answer XML can speak it.

**Prior work**
This builds directly on the already-merged Plivo SMS provider (#7584). Same provider, additive Voice Call option, SMS unchanged so existing configurations keep working.

**Notes**
Opening this for discussion first (per the contribution guide) before raising the PR. The implementation is ready, it extends `server/notification-providers/plivo.js`, the `Plivo.vue` form, and `src/lang/en.json`.
Happy to adjust the approach.

title: Feature Request: Embedded Script Engine for Custom Response Validation
author: Filippo125
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests


There are similar needs requested (and addressed) in: 

- #2431
- #4614

The most similar is:
- #1117

### 🔖 Feature description

Today Uptime Kuma provides several ways to validate HTTP responses:

-   Status Code
-   Keyword Match
-   JSON Query (JSONata)

These cover most common use cases, but there is still no flexible way to
express custom validation logic.

As a result, users often need to build external scripts and use Push
monitors for relatively simple validation scenarios.

## Real-world examples

### Dynamic timestamps

``` json
{
  "generatedAt": "2026-07-13T16:30:00Z"
}
```

The monitor should verify:

    generatedAt >= now - 15 minutes

### Plain text responses

Some services expose plain text instead of JSON.

Example:

    service-api-prod|2026-07-13T16:30:00Z
    service-worker|offline
    database|ok

The monitor should:

-   iterate over each line
-   find the line beginning with `service-api-`
-   extract the timestamp
-   compare it against the current time

### Business rules

Other common scenarios include:

-   compare multiple fields
-   aggregate values
-   validate ordering
-   parse proprietary text formats
-   perform calculations before deciding UP/DOWN

These cases are difficult or impossible using Keyword or JSON Query
alone.

### ✔️ Solution

## Proposal

Introduce a new monitor type:

> **Custom Validation Script**

The monitor executes a user-defined validation function inside an
embedded scripting engine.

The script only receives the HTTP response and the monitor configurations and returns the validation
result.

It **cannot** perform HTTP requests or access the operating system.

## Proposed Script API

``` javascript
function check(response, params, context) {
    return {
        ok: true,
        message: "Everything looks good"
    };
}
```

### Input

``` ts
response = {
    status: number,
    headers: Record<string,string>,
    body: string,
    durationMs: number
}

params = {
    ...
}

context = {
    now: number
}
```

### Output

``` ts
{
    ok: boolean,
    message?: string,
    metrics?: Record<string,number>
}
```

Only `ok` determines the monitor status.

## Execution Environment

The scripting environment should expose only:

-   response
-   params
-   context
-   console (optional)

No access to:

-   filesystem
-   network
-   environment variables
-   operating system
-   timers

This keeps the feature focused on response validation rather than
general scripting.

## Architecture

``` text
HTTP Request
      │
      ▼
Response
      │
      ▼
Embedded Script Engine
      │
      ▼
User Validation Function
      │
      ▼
{ ok, message }
      │
      ▼
Monitor Result
```

The monitor itself remains responsible for:

-   performing the HTTP request
-   timeout handling
-   authentication
-   redirects
-   retries

The script is responsible only for interpreting the response.

## Script Engine Abstraction

Rather than coupling the implementation to a specific scripting runtime,
the scripting layer could be exposed through an internal abstraction.

Conceptually:

``` ts
interface ScriptEngine {
    execute(
        script,
        response,
        params,
        context
    ): CheckResult;
}
```

This allows the underlying engine to evolve independently from the
monitor implementation.

Possible implementations could include lightweight embedded runtimes
such as QuickJS-NG, Duktape, or others, without affecting the public API
or stored monitor configuration.

## Why not JSONata?

JSONata is an excellent solution for querying structured JSON.

This proposal addresses a different problem:

-   arbitrary text parsing
-   proprietary formats
-   reusable validation logic
-   calculations
-   complex business rules

The goal is to complement JSONata, not replace it.

## Benefits

-   reduces the need for external Push monitors
-   enables advanced validation directly inside Kuma
-   works for both JSON and plain text
-   keeps the scripting API intentionally small
-   allows future evolution of the embedded runtime without changing
    monitor behavior

## Possible Future Enhancements

-   Script validation when saving
-   Built-in editor with syntax highlighting
-   Shared validation libraries
-   Helper functions
-   TypeScript definitions
-   Optional reusable scripts

## Implementation Notes

One possible implementation would be a lightweight embedded JavaScript
engine (for example QuickJS-NG), but this proposal intentionally focuses
on the monitor API rather than prescribing a specific runtime. The
scripting engine should be considered an implementation detail behind
the `ScriptEngine` abstraction.

### ❓ Alternatives

_No response_

### 📝 Additional Context

_No response_

title: Make Quick Stats counts (Down / Paused / etc.) clickable to open the filtered monitor list
author: plittlefield
body: ### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests I searched open and closed issues for existing requests covering this. The closest related ones are #3448 (show monitor group in Quick Stats), #2298 (count monitors with notifications disabled in Quick Stats) and #7421 (exclude monitor groups from Quick Stats Up count) - these are all about what the Quick Stats *numbers* count, not about the numbers being clickable/linked to a filtered view, so I believe this is a distinct request. Apologies also for issue #7581, which was auto-closed by the bot because I created it without a label attached (my mistake, not a template issue) - this is a clean resubmission of the same request with the label applied. ### 🔖 Feature description On the Dashboard, the "Quick Stats" row shows counts for Up, Down, Maintenance, Unknown and Pause. Today these numbers are static text. To see which monitors are Down or Paused, you have to separately open the "Status" dropdown filter above the monitor list and select the matching option. ### ✔️ Solution Make each number in Quick Stats a clickable link that applies the equivalent Status filter to the monitor list - e.g. clicking the "Down" count filters the list to Down monitors, clicking "Pause" filters to Paused monitors, and so on. This would just be a shortcut to the existing Status filter behaviour, not a new filtering feature. ### ❓ Alternatives Using the existing Status dropdown filter manually - it works, but with a long monitor list it's a couple of extra clicks versus a direct link from the number itself. ### 📝 Additional Context None.

title: Add NTRIP Caster Monitor Type
author: ByrdyB
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

This topic is similar to 
https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/7560
I have been working on this for a while and am just now getting around to creating an issue. The problem is the same but my solution differs a little.

### 🔖 Feature description

I need a way to monitor my NTRIP streams from my RTK GNSS base station that are being sent through a caster either private, which could be a self hosted [SNIP](https://www.use-snip.com/) instance or a public shared caster like [Emlid Caster](https://emlid.com/ntrip-caster/) or [RTK2GO](https://emlid.com/ntrip-caster/). NTRIP stands for Networked Transport of RTCM via Internet Protocol meaning that is carries GPS/GNSS RTK correction data over the internet as opposed to older RF systems. Many people, like me, rely heavily on very precise GPS systems that when go down, have to sit and wait until the problem gets fixed. This would give me an immediate warning that the system has stopped streaming and that i have very limited time to get it back going again before RTK fill has expired. Sorry if this was too much info but I was hoping to explain a little better why exactly this is so important to me.

### ✔️ Solution

What I am picturing is Uptime Kuma reaching out to the caster and logging into the stream. Once the connection is made hold the connection stays open until the monitor is paused, stopped, deleted, or some external force stops the connection in which case the connection needs gracefully cleaned up so a new connection can be attempted. I know this is quite a bit different that any other monitor type where the connection is made then disconnected on every status check. I considered doing it this way but that method will cause a lot of issues with some casters especially ones running SNIP software. By default SNIP has a guard against frequent connections stemming from something malicious or just a misconfigured rover/client. After several connections that last a short time >1 sec, the  software automatically bans the IP address of the connecting device and wont allow any more connection attempts until after a timeout period which would completely break the purpose of this monitor. The way I'm suggesting that we get around this issue is to keep the stream flowing and on the Uptime Kuma side, probe the stream every set interval to see if the data is still flowing fresh correction data. Another this we can do while probing is parse the data to tell what is contained to keep an eye on the quality of corrections contained. I am very open to suggestions on how to exactly implement the method of keeping the stream open while still polling the flowing data, because I'm not too sure the BEST method to make that happen.

### ❓ Alternatives

I actually tried the method of creating a new connection for every poll and caused issues with the casters I was using and is generally a bad practice for public casters managing thousand of connections.

### 📝 Additional Context

_No response_

title: Ignore status in group
author: kuitog
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

None


### 🔖 Feature description

I have some devices that are plugged on demand.
To organize them: they belong to a group, however they should be ignored for group uptime or group notifications.

Let me give you an example:
Group is: cameras
I have a mobile camera that I only plug when needed.

It is always off unless needed.
So: it belongs to the group but it shouldn't affect the group online status or notifications. I rely on a standalone notification for that specific device.


Another example: an ipad that has an automation to connect twice every 24 hrs to synchronize but it is usually offline to save battery.

Thanks!


### ✔️ Solution

.

### ❓ Alternatives

.

title: Add Plivo as a notification provider
author: sarveshpatil-plivo
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

I could not find an existing Plivo notification provider. New notification provider request.

### 🔖 Feature description

Add Plivo as an SMS notification provider. Plivo is a communications platform for sending SMS, so Uptime Kuma users who already use it could get monitor alerts by text message.

### ✔️ Solution

Add a notification provider that sends an SMS through the Plivo Messages API. It needs an Auth ID, an Auth Token, a from number, and a to number. It follows the standard notification-provider structure already used in the project. The implementation is ready and I can open a pull request with the required test screenshots.

### ❓ Alternatives

Teams that send their SMS through Plivo currently have no native option and would have to route alerts through a different service.

### 📝 Additional Context

_No response_

title: Bump protobufjs to ≥7.5.5 (CVE-2026-41242, critical RCE)
author: AKhozya
body:
`package.json` pins `"protobufjs": "~7.2.6"` — a direct dependency, used with `@grpc/grpc-js` for the gRPC monitor type. protobufjs `< 7.5.5` is affected by **CVE-2026-41242** / GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg (CVSS 8.8, arbitrary code execution via injected protobuf definition type fields).

The lockfile resolves **7.2.6** on both the `2.4.0` tag and `master`, so current releases and `master` ship the vulnerable version. An in-cluster trivy-operator scan flags it in `louislam/uptime-kuma:2.4.0-rootless`.

Fix: bump the range to `^7.5.5` (stays in the 7.x line, compatible with the current `@grpc/grpc-js ~1.8.22`) and refresh the lockfile.

Advisory: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-xq3m-2v4x-88gg

title: Configurable concurrency limit / batched execution for monitor checks
author: IDN-YN
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

[#3447](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/pull/3447) (closed PR implementing a similar Croner + Queue approach, never merged), [#6486](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/6486), [#6855](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/6855), [#6040](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/6040), [#5933](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues/5933)

### 🔖 Feature description

When a single Uptime Kuma instance manages many monitors (50-200+), all monitors sharing the same interval fire their checks at (nearly) the same moment. This causes CPU/network spikes on the monitored hosts, and on the Uptime Kuma side it has repeatedly led to database connection pool exhaustion (see the related issues above). There is currently no way to spread checks out over time — every monitor runs on its own independent timer with no shared concurrency limit.

### ✔️ Solution

Add an optional, configurable concurrency limit (or "batch size") for monitor checks — e.g. "check at most N monitors at the same time, queue the rest." A higher value checks more monitors simultaneously (faster overall, more resource usage); a lower value processes them in batches/waves (e.g. 10 at a time, then the next 10), reducing load spikes at the cost of some monitors being checked slightly later than their exact interval.

This was already proposed and implemented in [#3447](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/pull/3447) ("Change the monitor scheduler from setTimeout to Croner + Queue"), which added exactly this kind of configurable queue/concurrency setting, but that PR was closed without merging. It would be great to revisit this approach, or an alternative implementation of the same idea.

### ❓ Alternatives

- Manually staggering monitor creation/resume times — fragile, resets on every restart since all monitors resume simultaneously.
- Giving each monitor a slightly different interval so they drift out of phase — imprecise and doesn't survive restarts either.
- Splitting monitors across multiple Uptime Kuma instances — works but adds operational overhead just to solve a scheduling problem.

### 📝 Additional Context

none

title: Feature Request: Dependency Graph with Root Cause Analysis and Business Service Monitoring
author: RILuse
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

I found several related feature requests:

- #1089 – Dependent monitoring of hierarchical monitors
- #2261 – Parent/Child relation for better notifications
- #2335 – Dependent check
- #2487 – Conditional monitoring
- #4851 – More nesting and parent/child options

These requests mainly focus on adding a "Parent Monitor" or suppressing notifications when a parent host is down.

My proposal is broader.

Instead of introducing only parent/child relationships, I propose introducing a dependency graph with root cause analysis and business service monitoring.

The goal is not only to suppress duplicate alerts, but also to allow Uptime Kuma to explain *why* a service is unavailable and identify the actual root cause.

### 🔖 Feature description

Introduce dependency graphs between monitors together with root cause analysis and business service monitoring.

Today, every monitor is evaluated independently.

In real production environments, services are composed of multiple infrastructure components.

For example:

```text
1C ERP
├── SQL Server
├── HAProxy
├── IIS
└── Redis
```

If SQL Server becomes unavailable, Uptime Kuma currently reports:

❌ SQL Server

❌ 1C Web

❌ API

While technically correct, this does not answer the question operators usually ask:

> Why is my 1C service unavailable?

The same problem exists in network monitoring.

```text
Internet
    │
MikroTik
    │
Switch
    │
Hypervisor
├── VM01
├── VM02
└── SQL
```

If the router fails, every downstream monitor generates alerts even though they all have the same root cause.

### ✔️ Solution

I would like to introduce monitor dependencies instead of only independent checks.

Each monitor could optionally depend on one or more other monitors.

Example:

```text
Business Service
        │
     1C ERP
        │
 ┌──────┼────────┐
 SQL   HAProxy   IIS
```

or

```text
Internet
    │
MikroTik
    │
Switch
    │
Server
```

When a dependency is already DOWN:

- child monitors should either skip execution or be marked as **UNREACHABLE**
- child monitors should not generate notifications
- child monitors should not negatively affect uptime statistics
- only the root cause should trigger alerts

A new monitor state could be introduced:

- 🟢 UP
- 🔴 DOWN
- 🟡 UNREACHABLE (dependency unavailable)

Additionally, Uptime Kuma could display a simple dependency tree showing the root cause.

Example:

```text
❌ SQL Server

↳ 1C ERP

↳ API

↳ Reporting
```

or

```text
❌ MikroTik

↳ Switch

↳ Hypervisor

↳ Storage

↳ VM01

↳ VM02
```

This would allow operators to immediately understand why a service is unavailable instead of seeing dozens of independent alerts.

### ❓ Alternatives

I have considered using existing parent monitor proposals or external automation.

However, those solutions mainly suppress notifications and do not solve the broader problem.

What is often needed in production is understanding service dependencies rather than simply monitoring individual hosts.

Another option is using enterprise monitoring systems such as Zabbix or Checkmk, which already support dependency trees and root cause analysis.

However, one of Uptime Kuma's greatest strengths is its simplicity.

I believe introducing lightweight dependency graphs would significantly improve Uptime Kuma while preserving its ease of use.

### 📝 Additional Context

I work with enterprise environments where applications consist of multiple infrastructure components rather than a single endpoint.

For example:

```text
Business Service: 1C ERP

Dependencies:

✓ IIS
✓ HAProxy
✗ SQL Server

Status:

❌ 1C ERP

Reason:

SQL Server is unavailable
```

Another common example is network monitoring:

```text
Internet
    │
Firewall
    │
Core Switch
    │
Servers
```

If the firewall fails, operators are interested in the firewall outage—not receiving 20 notifications that every server is also unreachable.

I believe this feature would allow Uptime Kuma to evolve from an uptime checker into a lightweight infrastructure and business service monitoring platform while remaining simple to configure.

Thank you for considering this idea.

title: New security report
author: GigsTaggart

title: empty
author: GigsTaggart

title: Monitoring NTRIP caster service
author: redfast00
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

No issues found.

### 🔖 Feature description

(From Wikipedia): NTRIP is a protocol for streaming satellite navigation (GNSS) data over the Internet. NTRIP is a generic, stateless protocol based on the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP/1.1) based on the RTCM SC-104 protocol for GNSS data streams.

It's used to correct data in GPS receivers, so that they can be sub-centimeter accurate.

Problem is that if the NTRIP caster server goes down, you also lose out on precise GPS location. My government provides a free NTRIP service (well, actually, they provide 3, don't ask, it's complicated politically); but the uptime seems to be quite bad. It would be nice to have some hard numbers to show them and ask to improve.

### ✔️ Solution

There would be an option to monitor NTRIP services. Unlike HTTP, the connections are long-lived; you also need to provide authentication (like Basic Auth on http) and a NMEA sentence. The uptime monitor would also need to monitor that messages are actually being passed.

### ❓ Alternatives

I have considered just writing a script to monitor the service myself and then use the passive HTTP push feature. I guess that could also work.

### 📝 Additional Context

_No response_

title: Expose "Down since" and "was down for"
author: TheKalpit
body:
### ⚠️ Please verify that your question has not already been reported

- [x] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20sort%3Acreated-desc%20) and found no similar reports.


### 🛡️ Security Policy

- [x] I have read and agree to Uptime Kuma's [Security Policy](https://github.com/louislam/uptime-kuma/security/policy).


### 📝 Describe your problem

Not sure if this is already possible, but is there a way to add timestamps (since down detected, was down for) in message templates?

Here's the use-case:

A monitor tracking uptime of a service, every 5 minutes, and sends notification after 3 retries.
If the service goes down at 10:00 -> the notification will be sent at 10:15.
- is there a way to add "10:00" in this notification message?

Then later, say at 11:00 this service is up again. Total downtime: 1 hour.
- is there a way to add "[service] is up, was down for 1 hour" in the notification message?

### 📝 Error Message(s) or Log

```bash session

```

### 🐻 Uptime-Kuma Version

2.4.0

### 💻 Operating System and Arch

Ubuntu Server 24

### 🌐 Browser

Chrome

### 🖥️ Deployment Environment

- **Runtime Environment**:
  - Docker: Version `X.X.X` (Build `Y.Y.Y`)
  - Docker Compose: Version `X.X.X`
  - Portainer (BE/CE): Version `X.X.X` (LTS: Yes/No)
  - MariaDB: Version `X.X.X` (LTS: Yes/No)
  - Node.js: Version `X.X.X` (LTS: Yes/No)
  - Kubernetes (K3S/K8S): Version `X.X.X` (LTS: Yes/No, via `[method/tool]`)
- **Database**:
  - SQLite: Embedded
  - MariaDB: Embedded/External
- **Database Storage**:
  - **Filesystem**:
    - Linux: ext4/XFS/Btrfs/ZFS/F2FS
    - macOS: APFS/ HFS+
    - Windows: NTFS/ReFS
  - **Storage Medium**: HDD/eMMC/SSD/NVMe
- **Uptime Kuma Setup**:
  - Number of monitors: `X`

title: Add check availability button on each monitor
author: Spajki001
body:
### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests

Unable to find anything related.

### 🔖 Feature description

You should add, next to the pause/resume button on each monitor page, a check button that forces a manual health/availability check.

### ✔️ Solution

I have multiple monitors of different server and sometimes they go offline because of the internet instability or power outage. When they are back online, I have to pause and then resume each of those monitors to trigger a forced health/availability check. I would love it if you could just add the above mentioned button to solve this.

### ❓ Alternatives

_No response_

### 📝 Additional Context

_No response_

title: Domain Expiration add support for *.co.jp domains
author: procs111
body: ### 📑 I have found these related issues/pull requests i couldnt find anything similar.. only issues/pull requests to hide the issue. ### 🔖 Feature description Currently domain expiry dashboard display and thus email notifications are not working with .co.jp domains. I think it's because JPRS use an older method. Is there a way to have a fall back to the old whois to get the expiry? eg. running **whois -h whois.jprs.jp "yahoo.co.jp/e"** the expiry date is under [State] [State] Connected (**2026/09/30**) ### ✔️ Solution 1. show the expiry date for .co.jp domains on the dashboard 2. send an email notification for the domain expiry like for the other TLD's. ### ❓ Alternatives _No response_ ### 📝 Additional Context _No response_
